notes: Recording police in public is generally allowed in Australia as long as it does not interfere with their duties.
Home CCTV is generally legal in Australia, especially when used to monitor your own property. Privacy issues arise if cameras capture neighbouring properties or private areas.
Employee monitoring is legal in Australia if it is transparent and justified, but covert or excessive monitoring can breach workplace laws.
Audio recording laws in Australia vary by state, but generally require consent from at least one or all parties depending on the jurisdiction.
Recording video in public places is generally legal in Australia, provided it does not breach privacy expectations or involve harassment.
Recording phone calls in Australia depends on state laws, with some requiring consent from all parties and others allowing one-party consent in limited situations.
GPS tracking laws in Australia depend on context, but secretly tracking someone without consent can be unlawful under surveillance or stalking laws.
Drone use is legal in Australia under aviation rules, but using drones to monitor people or property can raise privacy concerns.
Businesses in Australia can use CCTV for security and safety, but must comply with privacy and workplace surveillance laws.
Hidden cameras are not automatically illegal in Australia, but using them without consent in private settings can breach surveillance laws.
